name: Coconut Chutney
description: Coconut Chutney is a classic South Indian condiment made from freshly grated coconut blended with roasted chana dal, green chillies and ginger, tempered with whole spices, urad dal, curry leaves, dried red chillies and asafoetida. It is a creamy, flavorful accompaniment to traditional dishes like Idli, Dosa, Uttapam, Medu Vada and other South Indian delicacies.

steps.instruction: Add freshly grated coconut, green chillies, ginger, roasted chana dal and salt to a chutney grinder jar.
steps.tip: Use freshly broken and scraped coconut for best tasting chutney. Avoid dried up coconut.

steps.instruction: Add 3 to 4 tablespoons water and grind to a fine and smooth consistency. Add more water if needed.
steps.tip: If using desiccated coconut, use warm water while grinding to get the right consistency.

steps.instruction: Remove the chutney into a heat-proof bowl. Check taste and add more salt if needed.
Heat oil in a small tadka pan over low heat. Add mustard seeds and let them crackle.
Add urad dal and cumin seeds. Fry till urad dal turns golden and cumin crackles.
Add dried red chilli, curry leaves and asafoetida. Fry for a few seconds till curry leaves become crisp.
Switch off the flame and immediately pour the entire tempering over the chutney.
Mix very well and serve fresh with idli, dosa, vada, pongal or pakoda.
steps.tip: Coconut chutney is best consumed fresh. Refrigerate if needed and consume within a day.

tips: Use freshly broken and scraped coconut for best tasting chutney. Avoid dried up coconut.
If the coconut is a little tender, the chutney will taste even better.
Roasted chana dal adds a lovely nutty taste. Skip if unavailable — the chutney will still be delicious.
For a tangier chutney, add ½ to 1 tsp tamarind or 2 to 3 tbsp fresh curd before tempering.
If using desiccated coconut, use warm water while grinding to get the right consistency.
Coconut chutney is best consumed fresh. Refrigerate if needed and consume within a day.
